license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.notice: Event descriptions are paraphrased summaries written for this dataset. Source links point to authoritative references.

entries.title: Engine Lifecycle State Changed: Started
entries.details: Generated when a PowerShell engine session starts. The HostApplication field records the full command line used to invoke PowerShell, including any encoded arguments.

investigationPivots: HostApplication is the primary pivot — flag -EncodedCommand, -WindowStyle Hidden, -NonInteractive, or -ExecutionPolicy Bypass in the invocation string
HostApplication values showing invocation from wscript.exe, mshta.exe, cmd.exe, or Office processes are high-priority
Correlate with EID 403 to calculate session duration — very short sessions may indicate one-liner or fileless execution
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate administrative PowerShell sessions
Scheduled tasks and software agents invoking PowerShell for automation

keyFields.name: HostApplication
keyFields.description: Full command line used to invoke PowerShell, including arguments and encoded commands

keyFields.name: HostName
keyFields.description: Name of the PowerShell host process (e.g., ConsoleHost, ServerRemoteHost)

keyFields.name: EngineVersion
keyFields.description: Version of the PowerShell engine that started

entries.title: Engine Lifecycle State Changed: Stopped
entries.details: Generated when a PowerShell engine session ends. Records the same lifecycle metadata as EID 400 for the session that is stopping.

investigationPivots: Pair with EID 400 to calculate session duration — sub-second sessions are characteristic of one-liner payload execution
Missing EID 403 after EID 400 may indicate the engine was forcibly killed (e.g., by a security tool or crash)
commonFalsePositives: Any normal PowerShell session ending

keyFields.name: HostApplication
keyFields.description: Full command line of the PowerShell session that ended

keyFields.name: HostName
keyFields.description: Name of the PowerShell host process that stopped

entries.title: Command Started
entries.details: Generated at the start of each command execution in the classic Windows PowerShell log. Records the command name in the CommandLine field. High volume in normal operation.

investigationPivots: CommandLine field records the command name — use as a coarse filter to identify suspicious cmdlets, then correlate with EID 4103 for full parameter detail
Pair with EID 501 to calculate per-command execution time
commonFalsePositives: High-volume event during any PowerShell session — filter to specific command names of interest

keyFields.name: CommandLine
keyFields.description: Name of the command being executed

entries.title: Command Stopped
entries.details: Generated when a command execution ends in the classic Windows PowerShell log. Records the completion of the command started in EID 500.

investigationPivots: Pair with EID 500 to calculate per-command duration
On its own this event has low fidelity — value increases when correlated with EID 4103 or 4104
commonFalsePositives: Any normal command completion during a PowerShell session

keyFields.name: CommandLine
keyFields.description: Name of the command that completed execution

entries.title: Provider Lifecycle State Changed: Started
entries.details: Generated when a PowerShell provider is loaded into the session. The ProviderName field identifies which provider was loaded (e.g., WSMan, Registry, Certificate, FileSystem).

investigationPivots: WSMan provider loaded in a non-interactive or automated session may indicate PowerShell remoting activity
Registry and Certificate providers loaded in unexpected contexts warrant review of subsequent EID 4103/4104 for what was accessed
Correlate provider load order with EID 400 HostApplication to understand the session context
commonFalsePositives: All standard providers (FileSystem, Registry, Certificate, etc.) load during normal interactive PowerShell sessions

keyFields.name: ProviderName
keyFields.description: Name of the PowerShell provider that was loaded into the session

entries.title: Pipeline Execution Details
entries.details: Generated in the classic Windows PowerShell log with pipeline execution details including command name and pipeline ID. Lower fidelity than EID 4103 as it does not capture full parameters or script content. Present on older systems where the operational log or module logging is not configured.

investigationPivots: Primarily useful on systems without EID 4103 module logging configured — treat as a fallback data source
Command name in this event can serve as a coarse filter; investigate further using other available telemetry
commonFalsePositives: Any pipeline execution in a PowerShell session

keyFields.name: CommandLine
keyFields.description: Name of the command executed in the pipeline

keyFields.name: PipelineId
keyFields.description: Identifier of the pipeline execution

entries.title: PowerShell Error
entries.details: Generated when an error occurs during pipeline execution in the operational log. The ErrorRecord field captures the error message, including command names, module names, and file paths involved in the failure.

investigationPivots: ErrorRecord field may expose command names, module names, or file paths associated with attacker tooling even when execution failed
Clusters of errors referencing known offensive cmdlets (e.g., Invoke-Mimikatz, Set-MpPreference, Add-MpPreference) are high-confidence attack indicators
Correlate with EID 4104 on the same ScriptBlockId to review the full script that generated the error
commonFalsePositives: Script errors in legitimate automation due to missing modules or version mismatches
Interactive users running commands with incorrect syntax

keyFields.name: ErrorRecord
keyFields.description: Full error message from the failed pipeline execution

keyFields.name: ScriptBlockId
keyFields.description: GUID linking to the EID 4104 script block that generated the error

entries.title: Module Logging
entries.summary: Module logging captured full pipeline execution with parameters.
entries.details: Generated when module logging is enabled via GPO (Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Windows PowerShell > Turn on Module Logging). Records the full command with all parameter values, pipeline input/output, and the user context. Higher fidelity than classic log EID 800. Requires GPO setting per module or wildcard (*).

investigationPivots: Full parameter values are recorded — look for credential strings, encoded payloads, or remote host names in parameter fields
SubjectUserName and HostApplication establish user and process context
Correlate with EID 4104 to link module-level execution to the script block that invoked it
commonFalsePositives: High-volume event in environments with module logging enabled — filter to specific modules or cmdlets of interest
Legitimate administrative scripts generating large volumes of module logging events

keyFields.name: Payload
keyFields.description: The pipeline execution detail or command output; search for download cradles, encoded strings, and suspicious cmdlets

keyFields.name: ContextInfo
keyFields.description: Contextual information including host application, runspace, and connected user

keyFields.name: HostName
keyFields.description: PowerShell host running the command (e.g., ConsoleHost, Windows PowerShell ISE); non-standard hosts are suspicious

keyFields.name: HostApplication
keyFields.description: Full path of the application hosting PowerShell; unexpected parents like wscript.exe or mshta.exe are red flags

detectionRules.title: PowerShell Module Logging — Suspicious Download Cradle or Obfuscation
detectionRules.rule:
Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-PowerShell"
| where EventID == 4103
| where RenderedDescription has_any (
    "IEX", "Invoke-Expression", "DownloadString", "DownloadFile",
    "WebClient", "Net.WebClient", "System.Net.WebClient",
    "FromBase64String", "[Convert]::", "EncodedCommand",
    "Invoke-Mimikatz", "Invoke-ReflectivePEInjection"
  )
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, UserName, RenderedDescription
detectionRules.notes: Module logging captures all pipeline execution. Payload field contains the command. High volume — tune with additional context filters like HostApplication.

detectionRules.title: PowerShell Module Logging Suspicious Keywords
detectionRules.rule:
index=wineventlog EventCode=4103
(Payload="*IEX*" OR Payload="*Invoke-Expression*" OR Payload="*DownloadString*"
OR Payload="*WebClient*" OR Payload="*FromBase64String*" OR Payload="*Invoke-Mimikatz*")
| table _time, Computer, User, Payload, ContextInfo
detectionRules.notes: Search the Payload field for known attacker keywords and download cradles.

detectionRules.title: PowerShell Module Logging Suspicious Activity
detectionRules.rule:
title: PowerShell Module Logging Suspicious Activity
status: experimental
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: powershell
detection:
    selection:
        EventID: 4103
        Payload|contains:
            - 'IEX'
            - 'Invoke-Expression'
            - 'DownloadString'
            - 'Net.WebClient'
            - 'FromBase64String'
            - 'Invoke-Mimikatz'
            - 'Invoke-ReflectivePEInjection'
falsepositives:
    - Legitimate administration scripts
    - Software deployment tools
level: medium
detectionRules.notes: Baseline keyword list; expand with environment-specific attacker TTPs.

entries.title: Script Block Logging
entries.details: Generated when script block logging is enabled (GPO: Turn on PowerShell Script Block Logging). Captures the full text of every compiled script block after deobfuscation, meaning even obfuscated or encoded scripts appear in cleartext. Level 3 (Warning) is generated automatically without GPO when AMSI flags a script block as suspicious. Large scripts are split across multiple sequential events sharing a MessageNumber/MessageTotal field.

prerequisites.description: Script Block Logging must be enabled via Group Policy ('Turn on PowerShell Script Block Logging'). Exception: Level 3 (Warning) events for AMSI-flagged suspicious scripts are generated automatically without GPO configuration.

investigationPivots: ScriptBlockText is post-deobfuscation — even heavily encoded or concatenated scripts appear in cleartext here
Warning-level (3) events are AMSI-triggered and generated regardless of GPO configuration — always treat these as high priority
MessageNumber/MessageTotal fields identify fragments of a split script — reassemble in order for full content
Search ScriptBlockText for known IOCs: download cradles (Net.WebClient, IEX, Invoke-Expression), credential access (mimikatz, sekurlsa), and lateral movement cmdlets
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate security tools and scripts that use encoding or obfuscation for intellectual property protection
AMSI-triggered Warning events from security testing or red team activity in authorized environments

keyFields.name: ScriptBlockText
keyFields.description: Full text of the script block being executed; the primary field for detecting malicious PowerShell content

keyFields.name: Path
keyFields.description: Path of the script file if executed from disk; empty means executed interactively or from memory

keyFields.name: ScriptBlockId
keyFields.description: GUID uniquely identifying the script block; large scripts are split across multiple events with the same ID

detectionRules.title: PowerShell Script Block — Attacker Toolkit Keywords
detectionRules.rule:
Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-PowerShell"
| where EventID == 4104
| where RenderedDescription has_any (
    "Invoke-Mimikatz", "Invoke-ReflectivePEInjection", "Invoke-Shellcode",
    "PowerSploit", "Get-GPPPassword", "Invoke-CredentialInjection",
    "AmsiUtils", "amsiInitFailed", "System.Management.Automation.AmsiUtils",
    "-EncodedCommand", "-enc ", "[System.Convert]::FromBase64String",
    "VirtualAlloc", "WriteProcessMemory", "CreateThread"
  )
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, UserName, RenderedDescription
detectionRules.notes: Script block logging captures full script content even for obfuscated scripts (AMSI sees decoded content). Higher fidelity than 4103 for detecting known tools.

detectionRules.title: PowerShell Script Block Logging — Malicious Content
detectionRules.rule:
index=wineventlog EventCode=4104
(ScriptBlock_Text="*Invoke-Mimikatz*" OR ScriptBlock_Text="*AmsiUtils*"
OR ScriptBlock_Text="*amsiInitFailed*" OR ScriptBlock_Text="*FromBase64String*"
OR ScriptBlock_Text="*VirtualAlloc*" OR ScriptBlock_Text="*Invoke-ReflectivePEInjection*")
| table _time, Computer, User, ScriptBlock_Text, Path
detectionRules.notes: The ScriptBlock_Text field contains the full decoded script content.

detectionRules.title: PowerShell Script Block Logging Malicious Keywords
detectionRules.rule:
title: PowerShell Script Block Logging Malicious Keywords
status: stable
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: powershell
detection:
    selection:
        EventID: 4104
        ScriptBlockText|contains:
            - 'Invoke-Mimikatz'
            - 'AmsiUtils'
            - 'amsiInitFailed'
            - 'Invoke-ReflectivePEInjection'
            - 'PowerSploit'
            - 'Get-GPPPassword'
            - 'VirtualAlloc'
            - 'WriteProcessMemory'
falsepositives:
    - Security tooling and EDR agents
    - Penetration testing
level: high
detectionRules.notes: High confidence rule; these strings rarely appear in legitimate scripts.

entries.title: Started Invocation
entries.details: Generated at the start of a script block invocation. The ScriptBlockId field links back to the EID 4104 event that captured the script content.

investigationPivots: ScriptBlockId links this event back to the EID 4104 script block content — use it to identify what was being executed
Correlate timestamp with network connections and Security EID 4688 process creation to build a full execution timeline
commonFalsePositives: Any legitimate script block invocation

keyFields.name: ScriptBlockId
keyFields.description: GUID linking to the EID 4104 event containing the script block content

entries.title: Completed Invocation
entries.details: Generated when a script block invocation ends. The ScriptBlockId field links back to the EID 4104 event that captured the script content.

investigationPivots: Pair with EID 4105 using ScriptBlockId to calculate execution duration
Missing EID 4106 after EID 4105 may indicate the runspace was killed, crashed, or hijacked — correlate with Application EID 1000
commonFalsePositives: Any legitimate script block completing normally

keyFields.name: ScriptBlockId
keyFields.description: GUID linking to the EID 4104 event containing the script block content

entries.title: PowerShell console host is starting up
entries.summary: An interactive PowerShell console (ConsoleHost) session is initialising.
entries.details: Generated at the start of an interactive PowerShell console session (powershell.exe or pwsh.exe launched interactively). This event distinguishes interactive console sessions from non-interactive script execution. A 40961 event fires for human-operated terminals but not for scripted automation, scheduled tasks, or WinRM-based remoting (which use ServerRemoteHost rather than ConsoleHost). The absence of 40961/40962 lifecycle events alongside 4103/4104 execution events indicates non-interactive (automated or remote) execution.

investigationPivots: Interactive console (40961) on a server where no interactive admin activity is expected indicates hands-on attacker presence — treat as high priority
40961 from a service account or non-admin user account is anomalous — interactive PS sessions should only be started by authorised administrators
Absence of 40961/40962 alongside 4103/4104 execution events confirms non-interactive execution — correlate with EID 400 HostApplication for invocation method
Correlate timestamp with Security EID 4648 (explicit credential logon) — interactive sessions started using RunAs or explicit credentials are higher fidelity
commonFalsePositives: Administrators opening PowerShell consoles interactively for legitimate administrative work
Developers using interactive PS sessions on workstations
IT automation testing run interactively before productising as a scheduled task

entries.title: PowerShell console host is ready for user input
entries.summary: An interactive PowerShell console (ConsoleHost) session is fully initialised and awaiting input.
entries.details: Generated when an interactive PowerShell ConsoleHost session completes initialisation and presents the prompt to the user. This event follows EID 40961 and confirms the interactive session is fully ready. The distinction between 40961 (starting) and 40962 (ready) allows calculation of console initialisation time.

investigationPivots: Pair with EID 40961 to confirm session lifecycle — an interactive console is open between these two events and EID 403
Correlate following 4103/4104 events with network connections (Sysmon EID 3) to build an attacker activity timeline
On servers, correlate with RDP session events (EID 4778/4779) to determine if the interactive PS session was opened over an RDP connection
commonFalsePositives: Any legitimate interactive PowerShell console session opened by an administrator

entries.title: PowerShell is authenticating to remote server
entries.summary: PowerShell is performing a WSMan/WinRM authentication handshake to a remote host.
entries.details: Generated when Windows PowerShell authenticates to a remote server via WSMan (WinRM/PSRemoting). This event fires on the initiating (client) machine and records outbound PowerShell remoting connections before session establishment. The Server field records the remote host, AuthMechanism records the authentication method (Kerberos, NTLM, Negotiate, Basic, CredSSP), and UserName records the account used.

investigationPivots: AuthMechanism = 'Basic' indicates cleartext credential transmission — this should never occur on production systems and is a critical configuration finding
AuthMechanism = 'CredSSP' delegates full credentials to the remote host — the remote host can then impersonate the user to any resource; flag unexpected CredSSP usage
Server (destination host) not matching expected management targets for the user account indicates lateral movement
NTLM PSRemoting (AuthMechanism = NTLM) to external or DMZ hosts may indicate data exfiltration staging via PSRemoting
Correlate with WinRM EID 6 on the destination to confirm session establishment and identify what was executed remotely
commonFalsePositives: IT administrators using Enter-PSSession for legitimate remote management
Ansible, DSC, or other automation frameworks using PSRemoting for configuration management
Monitoring solutions polling remote systems via PSRemoting

keyFields.name: Server
keyFields.description: Remote host being connected to; unexpected or external destinations are high risk

keyFields.name: AuthMechanism
keyFields.description: Authentication method; Basic = cleartext (critical), CredSSP = credential delegation (high risk), NTLM = relay risk

keyFields.name: UserName
keyFields.description: Account used for remote auth; service accounts or privileged accounts connecting to unusual hosts are anomalous

detectionRules.title: PowerShell Remoting with Basic or CredSSP Authentication
detectionRules.rule:
Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-PowerShell"
| where EventID == 53504
| where RenderedDescription has_any ("Basic", "CredSSP")
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, UserName, RenderedDescription
detectionRules.notes: Basic auth sends credentials in cleartext. CredSSP delegates credentials to the remote host. Both should be banned by policy and any occurrence investigated.

detectionRules.title: PowerShell Remoting with Unsafe Authentication Method
detectionRules.rule:
title: PowerShell Remoting Using Basic or CredSSP Authentication
status: stable
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: powershell
detection:
    selection:
        EventID: 53504
        AuthMechanism:
            - 'Basic'
            - 'CredSSP'
    condition: selection
falsepositives:
    - Legacy management scripts not yet migrated to Kerberos
    - Specific DSC pull server configurations using CredSSP
level: high
detectionRules.notes: Basic auth is cleartext and should alert as critical. CredSSP is high risk if used outside documented and approved scenarios.
